1

Electric Charge & Quantization

Lecture 0336

All electric phenomena begin with charge. Charge is a property of matter that comes in two kinds and only ever exists in whole-number multiples of one tiny amount.

Core Concepts

The Law of Charges: like charges repel and unlike charges attract. When you rub two materials together, electrons transfer from one to the other — the object that gains electrons becomes negative and the one that loses them becomes positive. (Rub rubber with fur → rubber goes negative; rub glass with silk → glass goes positive.)

Charge is carried by electrons and protons, each with charge magnitude equal to the elementary charge \(e\). The electron carries \(-e\) and the proton \(+e\). The proton is roughly 1836× more massive than the electron, and unlike the electron it is not elementary — it is built from quarks (up quark \(=+\tfrac{2}{3}e\), down quark \(=-\tfrac{1}{3}e\); a proton is \(uud=+e\), a neutron is \(udd=0\)).

Charge is quantized. Any object's net charge \(q\) must be an integer multiple of \(e\), because you cannot split a proton or electron into fractions.

Key Equations & Constants
Quantization of charge
\[ q = n e \]
  • \(q\) = net charge on the object (C)
  • \(n\) = number of excess charge carriers — must be an integer
  • \(e = 1.60\times10^{-19}\ \text{C}\) = elementary charge; the SI unit of charge is the coulomb (C)
Elementary charge
\(e = 1.60\times10^{-19}\ \text{C}\)
Electron mass
\(m_e = 9.11\times10^{-31}\ \text{kg}\)
Proton mass
\(m_p = 1.67\times10^{-27}\ \text{kg}\)
Mass ratio
\(m_p/m_e \approx 1836\)
Worked Examples
Example How many excess protons make 1 coulomb?

Solve \(q=ne\) for \(n\):

\[ n=\frac{q}{e}=\frac{1\ \text{C}}{1.60\times10^{-19}\ \text{C}}=6.25\times10^{18}\ \text{protons} \]

That's 6.25 quintillion protons — a reminder that one coulomb is an enormous amount of charge.

Example Can an object carry \(-2.00\times10^{-19}\,\text{C}\)?
\[ n=\frac{q}{e}=\frac{-2.00\times10^{-19}}{-1.60\times10^{-19}}=1.25\ \text{electrons} \]

No. \(n\) is not an integer, and you cannot have a quarter of an electron. Charge is quantized, so this net charge is impossible.

Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. An object has a net charge of \(-4.8\times10^{-19}\ \text{C}\). How many excess electrons does it carry?
  • A2
  • B3
  • C4
  • DIt is impossible to tell.
Answer B — 3 electrons

\( n=\dfrac{|q|}{e}=\dfrac{4.8\times10^{-19}}{1.60\times10^{-19}}=3 \). The object has 3 more electrons than protons.

MCQ
Q2. Which of the following is a possible net charge on an isolated object?
  • A\(3.2\times10^{-19}\ \text{C}\)
  • B\(2.4\times10^{-19}\ \text{C}\)
  • C\(4.0\times10^{-19}\ \text{C}\)
  • D\(5.6\times10^{-19}\ \text{C}\)
Answer A

A valid charge must be an integer multiple of \(e=1.60\times10^{-19}\,\text{C}\). Dividing each option by \(e\): A gives \(2.0\) ✓, B gives \(1.5\) ✗, C gives \(2.5\) ✗, D gives \(3.5\) ✗. Only \(3.2\times10^{-19}\,\text{C}=2e\) is allowed.

MCQ
Q3. A hypothetical particle is composed of two up quarks and one down quark. What is its net charge?
  • A\(0\)
  • B\(+\tfrac{1}{3}e\)
  • C\(+e\)
  • D\(-e\)
Answer C — this is a proton

\( q=2\left(+\tfrac{2}{3}e\right)+1\left(-\tfrac{1}{3}e\right)=+\tfrac{4}{3}e-\tfrac{1}{3}e=+e \). Two up quarks and one down quark (\(uud\)) is exactly the composition of a proton.

FRQ
Q4. A glass rod is rubbed with silk and transfers \(5.0\times10^{10}\) electrons to the silk. (a) State the sign and magnitude of the rod's resulting charge. (b) What is the charge on the silk, and what principle requires it?
Solution

(a) The rod loses electrons, so it becomes positive. Magnitude:

\[ q=ne=(5.0\times10^{10})(1.60\times10^{-19})=8.0\times10^{-9}\ \text{C}=+8.0\ \text{nC} \]

(b) The silk gains exactly those electrons, so its charge is \(-8.0\ \text{nC}\). This is required by conservation of charge: the total charge of the isolated rod-plus-silk system is unchanged, so the charges must be equal and opposite.

FRQ
Q5. A student claims to have measured a net charge of \(1.0\times10^{-19}\ \text{C}\) on an isolated oil droplet. Explain quantitatively why this measurement must be in error.
Solution

Charge is quantized: \(q=ne\) with \(n\) an integer. Testing the claim:

\[ n=\frac{q}{e}=\frac{1.0\times10^{-19}}{1.60\times10^{-19}}=0.625 \]

Since \(0.625\) is not an integer, no whole number of electrons or protons could produce this charge. The smallest possible non-zero charge magnitude on an isolated object is \(e=1.60\times10^{-19}\,\text{C}\), so the measured value is impossible.

2

Coulomb's Law — The Electric Force

Lecture 0337

The Law of Charges tells us the direction of the force between charges. Coulomb's Law gives us its magnitude.

Core Concepts

The electric (Coulomb / electrostatic) force between any two point charges acts along the line joining them. It has the same inverse-square form as Newton's law of gravitation, but the Coulomb constant \(k\) is enormous compared to \(G\) — about \(1.35\times10^{20}\) times larger — which is why electric forces dominate at the atomic scale.

A point charge is an object of negligible size carrying charge (the charge analog of a point mass). Useful prefixes: \(1\ \mu\text{C}=10^{-6}\,\text{C}\), \(1\ \text{nC}=10^{-9}\,\text{C}\), \(1\ \text{pC}=10^{-12}\,\text{C}\).

Sign convention used in this coursePlug in charges with their signs. A negative result means an attractive force; a positive result means a repulsive force. (Gravity, by contrast, is always attractive.) For more than two charges, the net force is the vector sum of the individual forces — superposition.

Key Equations & Constants
Coulomb's Law (magnitude & sign)
\[ F_e=\frac{k\,q_1 q_2}{r^2} \]
  • \(F_e\) = electric force between the charges (N)
  • \(q_1,q_2\) = the two charges, with sign (C)
  • \(r\) = distance between their centers of charge (m)
  • \(k=8.99\times10^{9}\ \dfrac{\text{N}\cdot\text{m}^2}{\text{C}^2}\) = Coulomb constant \(=\dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\)
Worked Examples
Example 1 Force between two opposite charges
\[ F_e=\frac{(8.99\times10^{9})(+5\times10^{-6})(-5\times10^{-6})}{(2.0)^2}=-0.056\ \text{N} \]

The negative sign means the force is attractive: each charge feels \(0.056\ \text{N}\) pulling it toward the other.

Example 2 Superposition with a third charge

The pull from \(q_2\) (\(0.056\,\text{N}\), to the right) is unchanged. The new charge \(q_3\) is positive like \(q_1\), so it repels \(q_1\) — also to the right:

\[ F_{31}=\frac{(8.99\times10^{9})(+5\times10^{-6})(+5\times10^{-6})}{(2.0)^2}=0.056\ \text{N (right)} \]
\[ \textstyle\sum F_{\text{on }1}=F_{21}+F_{31}=0.056+0.056=0.11\ \text{N to the right} \]
Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. Two point charges exert a force \(F\) on each other. If the distance between them is tripled (charges unchanged), the new force is:
  • A\(3F\)
  • B\(F/3\)
  • C\(F/9\)
  • D\(9F\)
Answer C

Since \(F\propto 1/r^2\), tripling \(r\) divides the force by \(3^2=9\). So \(F\to F/9\).

MCQ
Q2. The force between two point charges is \(F\). If both charges are doubled and the separation is also doubled, the new force is:
  • A\(2F\)
  • B\(F\) (unchanged)
  • C\(4F\)
  • D\(F/4\)
Answer B

\( F\propto \dfrac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}\). Doubling each charge multiplies the numerator by \(2\times2=4\); doubling \(r\) multiplies the denominator by \(2^2=4\). The factors cancel, so the force is unchanged.

MCQ
Q3. Point charges \(+2.0\ \mu\text{C}\) and \(+3.0\ \mu\text{C}\) are separated by \(0.10\ \text{m}\). The magnitude of the force on each is closest to:
  • A\(0.54\ \text{N}\)
  • B\(5.4\ \text{N}\)
  • C\(54\ \text{N}\)
  • D\(0.054\ \text{N}\)
Answer B
\[ F=\frac{(8.99\times10^{9})(2.0\times10^{-6})(3.0\times10^{-6})}{(0.10)^2}=\frac{0.0539}{0.010}\approx 5.4\ \text{N} \]

Both charges are positive, so the force is repulsive (and equal in magnitude on each, by Newton's third law).

FRQ
Q4. Three charges lie on the x-axis: \(q_1=+4.0\,\mu\text{C}\) at \(x=0\), \(q_2=-3.0\,\mu\text{C}\) at \(x=0.20\,\text{m}\), and \(q_3=+2.0\,\mu\text{C}\) at \(x=0.40\,\text{m}\). Find the magnitude and direction of the net force on \(q_3\).
Solution \(\approx 0.90\ \text{N}\) in the \(-x\) direction

Force from \(q_1\) on \(q_3\) (both +, repulsive → pushes \(q_3\) in \(+x\)), \(r=0.40\,\text{m}\):

\[ F_{13}=\frac{(8.99\times10^9)(4.0\times10^{-6})(2.0\times10^{-6})}{(0.40)^2}=0.45\ \text{N }(+x) \]

Force from \(q_2\) on \(q_3\) (opposite signs, attractive → pulls \(q_3\) toward \(q_2\), in \(-x\)), \(r=0.20\,\text{m}\):

\[ F_{23}=\frac{(8.99\times10^9)(3.0\times10^{-6})(2.0\times10^{-6})}{(0.20)^2}=1.35\ \text{N }(-x) \]

Net: \(\sum F = 0.45-1.35=-0.90\ \text{N}\). The net force is \(\mathbf{0.90\ N\ toward\ }q_2\) (the \(-x\) direction).

FRQ
Q5. Two identical small spheres each carry charge \(+Q\) and repel one another with a force of \(0.10\ \text{N}\) when their centers are \(0.50\ \text{m}\) apart. (a) Derive a symbolic expression for \(Q\). (b) Evaluate \(Q\) numerically.
Solution \(Q\approx 1.7\ \mu\text{C}\)

(a) With equal charges, \(F=\dfrac{kQ^2}{r^2}\Rightarrow Q=r\sqrt{\dfrac{F}{k}}\).

(b)

\[ Q=(0.50)\sqrt{\frac{0.10}{8.99\times10^{9}}}=(0.50)(3.34\times10^{-6})\approx 1.7\times10^{-6}\ \text{C} \]
3

Polarization of Charge

Lecture 0339

How can a charged balloon stick to a wall that has no net charge? Through polarization — a rearrangement, not a transfer, of charge.

Core Concepts

Polarization does not mean the neutral object becomes charged. It means the charges within its molecules shift slightly so that one side becomes a little more positive and the other a little more negative. When a negatively charged balloon is brought near a wall, the wall's electrons are pushed away (like charges repel), leaving the near surface slightly positive.

The key is distance. The unlike (attracting) charges end up closer together than the like (repelling) charges. Because Coulomb's Law goes as \(1/r^2\), the smaller \(r\) for the opposite charges makes the attractive force win:

Why there is a net attractionOpposite charges are nearer than like charges \(\Rightarrow\) by \(F\propto 1/r^2\), the attractive force exceeds the repulsive force \(\Rightarrow\) a neutral object is attracted to a charged one.

This is how a charged balloon picks up small pieces of paper or makes an aluminum can roll. The forces are tiny, but so are the masses involved. Polarization is generally stronger in conductors than insulators: in an insulator, electrons only shift slightly within each atom, but in a conductor they are free to migrate, producing a larger charge separation and a larger net attractive force.

Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. A positively charged rod is brought near (but not touching) a neutral conducting sphere. The surface of the sphere closest to the rod becomes:
  • Apositively charged
  • Bnegatively charged
  • Cneutral — nothing happens
  • Dalternately positive and negative
Answer B

The rod's positive charge attracts the sphere's free electrons toward the near surface, making it negative (and leaving the far side positive). The sphere's net charge is still zero — this is polarization/induction, not charging.

MCQ
Q2. A charged balloon is attracted to a neutral wall. The best explanation is that:
  • Acharge is transferred from the balloon to the wall, charging it oppositely
  • Bgravity pulls the balloon to the wall
  • Cthe wall's molecules polarize so opposite charges are closer than like charges
  • Dthe wall was already charged before the balloon arrived
Answer C

No net charge is transferred to the wall. Its molecules polarize; the nearer opposite charges produce a larger attractive force than the more-distant like charges (because \(F\propto 1/r^2\)), so the net force is attractive.

MCQ
Q3. Polarization-based attraction is usually stronger for a conductor than an insulator because in a conductor:
  • Afree electrons can migrate farther, creating a larger charge separation
  • Bconductors gain a net charge but insulators do not
  • Cconductors are always heavier
  • DCoulomb's Law does not apply to insulators
Answer A

In an insulator electrons only shift slightly within each atom. In a conductor they move freely and end up farther from the like charges, producing a greater difference between the attractive and repulsive forces — hence a larger net attraction.

FRQ
Q4. Using the Law of Charges and Coulomb's Law, explain carefully why a single charged balloon can attract a small piece of neutral paper.
Solution

The charged balloon's field polarizes the paper: charges within the paper's molecules shift so the side facing the balloon carries charge opposite to the balloon, and the far side carries like charge. By the Law of Charges, the near (opposite) charges attract the balloon while the far (like) charges repel it. Because these opposite charges are closer to the balloon than the like charges, and \(F\propto 1/r^2\), the attractive force is larger in magnitude than the repulsive force. The net electric force on the paper is therefore toward the balloon. The paper's net charge remains zero throughout — only its charge distribution changed.

4

Conservation of Charge

Lecture 0340

Charge is never created or destroyed — only moved around.

Core Concepts

Conservation of charge: the total electric charge of an isolated system never changes. Add up all the positive charge and subtract all the negative charge, and the total stays constant. The system can be as large as the universe or as small as an isolated electroscope.

Identical conductors that touchWhen two identical conducting spheres touch and separate, they share the total charge equally: \[ q_{1f}=q_{2f}=\frac{q_{1i}+q_{2i}}{2} \] Any valid final charge must also be an integer multiple of \(e\).
Worked Examples
Example 1 Which final charges are possible?

Two conducting objects start with \(+3e\) and \(-6e\), collide, and separate. The total must stay at \(q_{\text{total}}=+3e+(-6e)=-3e\), and each final charge must be an integer multiple of \(e\). Testing the options:

  • (a) \(+4e,-7e\Rightarrow -3e\) ✓ and integers — possible
  • (b) \(+2e,-2e\Rightarrow 0\ne-3e\) — no
  • (c) \(-1.5e,-1.5e\Rightarrow -3e\) but half-integers — no (charge is quantized)
  • (d) \(-3.5e,+2.5e\Rightarrow -e\) — no (wrong total and not integers)
  • (e) \(+e,-4e\Rightarrow -3e\) ✓ and integers — possible

Only (a) and (e) conserve charge with integer multiples of \(e\).

Example 2 Identical spheres touch — does the force change?

Identical spheres start at \(+3.0\,\text{pC}\) and \(+6.0\,\text{pC}\), a fixed distance \(x\) apart. They are touched together and returned to distance \(x\).

(a) Final charge on each: \(q_f=\dfrac{+3+6}{2}\,\text{pC}=+4.5\,\text{pC}\).

(b) Compare forces (\(k\) and \(x\) constant, so \(F\propto q_1q_2\)):

\[ F_{ei}\propto(3)(6)=18,\qquad F_{ef}\propto(4.5)(4.5)=20.25 \]

Since \(20.25>18\), the final electric force is greater than the initial force.

Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. Two identical conducting spheres carry \(+8.0\ \text{nC}\) and \(-2.0\ \text{nC}\). They are touched together and separated. The charge on each sphere afterward is:
  • A\(+6.0\ \text{nC}\)
  • B\(+10.0\ \text{nC}\)
  • C\(+3.0\ \text{nC}\)
  • D\(-3.0\ \text{nC}\)
Answer C

Total charge \(=+8.0+(-2.0)=+6.0\ \text{nC}\) is conserved. Identical spheres share it equally: \(q_f=\dfrac{+6.0}{2}=+3.0\ \text{nC}\) each.

MCQ
Q2. Sphere A carries \(+6.0\,\mu\text{C}\) and is touched to an identical neutral sphere B. The charge on B afterward is:
  • A\(0\)
  • B\(+3.0\,\mu\text{C}\)
  • C\(+6.0\,\mu\text{C}\)
  • D\(-3.0\,\mu\text{C}\)
Answer B

Total \(=+6.0+0=+6.0\,\mu\text{C}\), shared equally: each ends at \(+3.0\,\mu\text{C}\).

MCQ
Q3. Two objects begin with charges \(+2e\) and \(+5e\), collide, and separate. Which final pair is possible?
  • A\(+3e,\ +4e\)
  • B\(+3.5e,\ +3.5e\)
  • C\(+1e,\ +5e\)
  • D\(+8e,\ -2e\)
Answer A

Total must remain \(+2e+5e=+7e\) using integer multiples of \(e\). A: \(3e+4e=7e\) ✓. B: correct total but half-integers ✗. C: \(6e\) ✗. D: \(6e\) ✗.

FRQ
Q4. Two identical conducting spheres carry \(+8.0\ \text{nC}\) and \(-4.0\ \text{nC}\), held a distance \(d\) apart. They are touched together and returned to the same separation \(d\). (a) Find the charge on each sphere afterward. (b) Find the ratio \(F_{\text{after}}/F_{\text{before}}\) of the force magnitudes, and state whether the force increased or decreased.
Solution \(+2.0\ \text{nC}\) each; ratio \(=\tfrac{1}{8}\) (decreased)

(a) Total \(=+8.0+(-4.0)=+4.0\ \text{nC}\), shared equally \(\Rightarrow +2.0\ \text{nC}\) on each.

(b) With \(d\) fixed, \(F\propto|q_1q_2|\):

\[ \frac{F_{\text{after}}}{F_{\text{before}}}=\frac{|(2.0)(2.0)|}{|(8.0)(-4.0)|}=\frac{4.0}{32}=\frac{1}{8} \]

The force magnitude drops to one-eighth. (It also changes from attractive, opposite signs, to repulsive, like signs.)

5

Bohr Model: Speed of the Electron

Lecture 0341

A classic application: the electric force supplies the centripetal force that keeps an electron in orbit around the proton.

The Setup

In the Bohr model of hydrogen, the electron travels in a circular orbit of radius \(r=5.29\times10^{-11}\ \text{m}\) around the proton. The only force on the electron is the electric force, directed inward toward the proton — so it is the centripetal force.

Worked Solution — find the orbital speed
Derivation Electric force = centripetal force

Set the Coulomb force (between charges \(+e\) and \(-e\)) equal to \(m_e v^2/r\):

\[ \frac{k e^2}{r^2}=\frac{m_e v^2}{r}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad v=\sqrt{\frac{k e^2}{m_e\,r}}=e\sqrt{\frac{k}{m_e\,r}} \]

Substituting \(k=8.99\times10^9\), \(e=1.60\times10^{-19}\,\text{C}\), \(m_e=9.11\times10^{-31}\,\text{kg}\), \(r=5.29\times10^{-11}\,\text{m}\):

\[ v=\sqrt{\frac{(8.99\times10^9)(1.60\times10^{-19})^2}{(9.11\times10^{-31})(5.29\times10^{-11})}}\approx 2.19\times10^{6}\ \text{m/s} \]

About \(0.7\%\) of the speed of light — fast, but non-relativistic enough for this model.

Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. In the Bohr model, what plays the role of the centripetal force on the orbiting electron?
  • Athe gravitational force between electron and proton
  • Bthe normal force
  • Cthe electric (Coulomb) force
  • Da magnetic force
Answer C

The attractive electric force between the \(+e\) proton and \(-e\) electron points inward and provides the centripetal force. (Gravity between them is roughly \(10^{39}\) times weaker and is neglected.)

MCQ
Q2. Using \(v=\sqrt{ke^2/(m_e r)}\), if the orbital radius were made four times larger, the electron's speed would become:
  • A\(4\times\) as large
  • Bhalf as large
  • C\(\tfrac14\) as large
  • Dunchanged
Answer B

\(v\propto 1/\sqrt{r}\). Increasing \(r\) by a factor of 4 multiplies \(v\) by \(1/\sqrt{4}=\tfrac12\).

FRQ
Q3. For the hydrogen atom (\(r=5.29\times10^{-11}\ \text{m}\), electron speed \(v=2.19\times10^{6}\ \text{m/s}\)), find (a) the magnitude of the electric force on the electron, and (b) the period of one orbit.
Solution \(F\approx 8.2\times10^{-8}\ \text{N}\); \(T\approx 1.5\times10^{-16}\ \text{s}\)

(a) \( F=\dfrac{ke^2}{r^2}=\dfrac{(8.99\times10^9)(1.60\times10^{-19})^2}{(5.29\times10^{-11})^2}\approx 8.2\times10^{-8}\ \text{N}. \)

(b) \( T=\dfrac{2\pi r}{v}=\dfrac{2\pi(5.29\times10^{-11})}{2.19\times10^{6}}\approx 1.5\times10^{-16}\ \text{s}. \)

6

Balloon Excess Charges Experiment

Lecture 0342

A full equilibrium problem: two charged balloons repel and hang at an angle. From the geometry we can back out how many excess electrons each carries.

The Problem

Two balloons, each of mass \(m=0.0018\ \text{kg}\), carry approximately equal excess charge and hang from a common point on strings of length \(L=0.39\ \text{m}\), each making angle \(\theta=21^\circ\) with the vertical. Find the average number of excess charges on each balloon.

Worked Solution
Solution Equilibrium & Coulomb's Law

Each balloon is in equilibrium under tension, gravity, and the horizontal electric repulsion. Resolving the tension:

\[ F_T\cos\theta=F_g=mg,\qquad F_T\sin\theta=F_e \]

Dividing the equations eliminates \(F_T\):

\[ F_e=mg\tan\theta=(0.0018)(9.8)\tan21^\circ\approx 6.8\times10^{-3}\ \text{N} \]

The center-to-center separation comes from the geometry, \(r=2L\sin\theta\):

\[ r=2(0.39)\sin21^\circ\approx 0.280\ \text{m} \]

Now apply Coulomb's Law with equal charges \(q\) and solve for \(q\):

\[ F_e=\frac{kq^2}{r^2}\ \Rightarrow\ q=\sqrt{\frac{F_e\,r^2}{k}}=\sqrt{\frac{(6.8\times10^{-3})(0.280)^2}{8.99\times10^9}}\approx 2.4\times10^{-7}\ \text{C} \]

Finally, the number of excess charges:

\[ n=\frac{q}{e}=\frac{2.4\times10^{-7}}{1.60\times10^{-19}}\approx 1.5\times10^{12}\ \text{excess charges} \]
Was gravity between the balloons negligible?Yes. The electric force is about \(2\times10^{12}\) times larger than the gravitational attraction between the two balloons, so ignoring balloon–balloon gravity is completely justified.
Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. For a single hanging, charged balloon in equilibrium, which relationship correctly gives the electric force?
  • A\(F_e=mg\cos\theta\)
  • B\(F_e=mg/\tan\theta\)
  • C\(F_e=mg\tan\theta\)
  • D\(F_e=mg\sin\theta\)
Answer C

From \(F_T\cos\theta=mg\) and \(F_T\sin\theta=F_e\), dividing gives \(\tan\theta=F_e/mg\), so \(F_e=mg\tan\theta\).

MCQ
Q2. Two balloons hang from the same point on strings of length \(L\), each at angle \(\theta\) from the vertical. Their center-to-center separation is:
  • A\(2L\sin\theta\)
  • B\(L\sin\theta\)
  • C\(2L\cos\theta\)
  • D\(L\tan\theta\)
Answer A

Each balloon sits a horizontal distance \(L\sin\theta\) from the vertical center line, on opposite sides, so the separation is \(r=2L\sin\theta\).

FRQ
Q3. Two identical balloons of mass \(m=2.0\ \text{g}\) hang from a common point on strings of length \(L=0.50\ \text{m}\), each making \(\theta=15^\circ\) with the vertical. (a) Find the electric force on each balloon. (b) Find the charge \(q\) on each (assume equal charges). Use \(g=9.8\ \text{m/s}^2\).
Solution \(F_e\approx 5.3\times10^{-3}\ \text{N}\); \(q\approx 2.0\times10^{-7}\ \text{C}\)

(a) \( F_e=mg\tan\theta=(2.0\times10^{-3})(9.8)\tan15^\circ\approx 5.3\times10^{-3}\ \text{N}. \)

(b) Separation \( r=2L\sin\theta=2(0.50)\sin15^\circ\approx 0.259\ \text{m}. \) Then

\[ q=\sqrt{\frac{F_e\,r^2}{k}}=\sqrt{\frac{(5.3\times10^{-3})(0.259)^2}{8.99\times10^9}}\approx 2.0\times10^{-7}\ \text{C}. \]
7

Electric Fields

Lecture 0467

Instead of asking "what force does charge A feel from charge B?", the field concept asks "what does the space around a charge look like?" — independent of any test charge placed in it.

Core Concepts

The electric field at a point is the electrostatic force per unit charge that a small positive test charge would feel there. A positive test charge is small enough not to measurably disturb the field it probes. Field direction is defined by the force on a positive test charge, so \(\vec E\) and the force on a positive charge point the same way (and opposite for a negative charge).

Key Equations
Definition of the electric field
\[ \vec E=\frac{\vec F_e}{q}\qquad [\,\text{N/C}\,] \]
Field of a point charge
\[ E=\frac{kq}{r^2} \]
Gravitational analogyThe field idea mirrors gravity: \( \vec g=\dfrac{\vec F_g}{m}\) and \( g=\dfrac{GM}{r^2}\). A point charge's field looks like a planet's gravitational field; the uniform field between parallel plates looks like \(g\) near a planet's surface.
Electric Field Line Rules
  • Point in the direction a small positive test charge would be pushed.
  • Line density is proportional to field strength (closer lines = stronger field).
  • Start on positive charge, end on negative charge (or run to/from infinity if charge is unbalanced).
  • Always leave/meet a charged conductor's surface perpendicularly.
  • Never cross one another.
Worked Example
Example Field and force near a point charge

Find the field \(0.30\ \text{m}\) from a \(+2.0\ \mu\text{C}\) charge, then the force on a \(-3.0\ \text{nC}\) charge placed there.

\[ E=\frac{kq}{r^2}=\frac{(8.99\times10^9)(2.0\times10^{-6})}{(0.30)^2}\approx 2.0\times10^{5}\ \text{N/C (away from the +charge)} \]
\[ F=|q|E=(3.0\times10^{-9})(2.0\times10^5)=6.0\times10^{-4}\ \text{N (toward the +charge)} \]

The force on the negative charge points opposite to \(\vec E\) — i.e., back toward the positive source.

Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. An electron is placed in a uniform electric field that points due east. The electric force on the electron points:
  • Aeast (same as the field)
  • Bwest (opposite the field)
  • Cthere is no force
  • Dperpendicular to the field
Answer B

\(\vec F=q\vec E\). For the electron \(q=-e<0\), so the force is opposite the field — to the west.

MCQ
Q2. Which statement about electric field lines is false?
  • AThey begin on positive charge and end on negative charge.
  • BWhere they are closer together, the field is stronger.
  • CThey meet a conductor's surface perpendicularly.
  • DTwo field lines may cross where the field is strong.
Answer D

Field lines never cross. If they did, the field would have two directions at one point, which is impossible. A, B, and C are all true rules.

MCQ
Q3. At a point \(0.20\ \text{m}\) from a \(+8.0\ \text{nC}\) point charge, the magnitude of the electric field is closest to:
  • A\(36\ \text{N/C}\)
  • B\(360\ \text{N/C}\)
  • C\(1.8\times10^{3}\ \text{N/C}\)
  • D\(1.8\times10^{4}\ \text{N/C}\)
Answer C
\[ E=\frac{kq}{r^2}=\frac{(8.99\times10^9)(8.0\times10^{-9})}{(0.20)^2}=\frac{71.9}{0.04}\approx 1.8\times10^{3}\ \text{N/C} \]
FRQ
Q4. A \(+5.0\ \text{nC}\) point charge sits at the origin. (a) Find the electric field magnitude and direction at a point \(0.20\ \text{m}\) to its right. (b) An electron is released from rest at that point. Find the magnitude and direction of its initial acceleration. (\(m_e=9.11\times10^{-31}\ \text{kg}\))
Solution \(E\approx 1.1\times10^{3}\ \text{N/C}\); \(a\approx 2.0\times10^{14}\ \text{m/s}^2\) toward the charge

(a) \( E=\dfrac{kq}{r^2}=\dfrac{(8.99\times10^9)(5.0\times10^{-9})}{(0.20)^2}\approx 1.1\times10^{3}\ \text{N/C} \), pointing away from the positive charge (to the right).

(b) Force \(F=eE=(1.60\times10^{-19})(1.1\times10^3)=1.8\times10^{-16}\ \text{N}\), directed toward the charge (left), since the electron is negative. Then

\[ a=\frac{F}{m_e}=\frac{1.8\times10^{-16}}{9.11\times10^{-31}}\approx 2.0\times10^{14}\ \text{m/s}^2\ \text{(toward the charge).} \]
8

Continuous Charge Distributions

Lecture 0468-1

Real charged objects aren't points. To find their fields we slice them into infinitely many tiny point charges \(dq\) and add up the contributions — an integral.

Core Concepts

A continuous charge distribution has shape and charge spread throughout it. We treat the object as an infinite number of infinitesimal point charges \(dq\), each contributing a field \(d\vec E\), and integrate (superposition):

\[ \vec E=\int d\vec E=\int \frac{k\,dq}{r^2}\,\hat r \]
What AP Physics C expectsYou should be able to set up and evaluate this integral for highly symmetric cases: an infinite line/cylinder (field at a distance from the axis), a ring of charge (on its axis), a semicircular arc (at its center), and a finite line of charge (along its axis or along the perpendicular bisector).
Charge Densities
DistributionDensityCharge element \(dq\)Units
Linear (wire/rod)\(\lambda=\dfrac{Q}{L}\)\(dq=\lambda\,dx\)C/m
Surface (sheet/plate)\(\sigma=\dfrac{Q}{A}\)\(dq=\sigma\,dA\)C/m²
Volume (solid)\(\rho=\dfrac{Q}{V}\)\(dq=\rho\,dV\)C/m³
Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. The SI units of linear charge density \(\lambda\) are:
  • A\(\text{C/m}^2\)
  • B\(\text{C/m}\)
  • C\(\text{C/m}^3\)
  • D\(\text{C}\)
Answer B

\(\lambda=Q/L\) is charge per length, so C/m. (Surface \(\sigma\) is C/m²; volume \(\rho\) is C/m³.)

MCQ
Q2. A rod of length \(L\) carries total charge \(Q\) spread uniformly. The charge in a small element of length \(dx\) is:
  • A\(dq=\dfrac{Q}{L}\,dx\)
  • B\(dq=\dfrac{Q}{L^2}\,dx\)
  • C\(dq=QL\,dx\)
  • D\(dq=\dfrac{L}{Q}\,dx\)
Answer A

Uniform linear density \(\lambda=Q/L\), so \(dq=\lambda\,dx=\dfrac{Q}{L}\,dx\).

MCQ
Q3. When integrating to find the field on the axis of a ring of charge, why does the integral simplify so much?
  • AThe charge elements all have different distances to the point.
  • BThe field is zero everywhere on the axis.
  • CBy symmetry, components perpendicular to the axis cancel, and every \(dq\) is the same distance from the point.
  • DThe charge density is not uniform.
Answer C

Symmetry kills the perpendicular components (they cancel in pairs), leaving only the axial component. And every point on the ring is an equal distance \(\sqrt{x^2+a^2}\) from the axial point, so that factor comes out of the integral.

9

Charged Rod — Field of a Finite Line

Lecture 0468-2

Our first full integration: the field at a point lying along the line of a uniformly charged rod.

The Problem

Find the electric field at point \(P\), a distance \(a\) from the near end of a thin rod of length \(L\), total charge \(+Q\), and uniform linear density \(\lambda=Q/L\), where \(P\) lies on the line of the rod.

Worked Derivation
Derivation Integrate the point-charge field

Every \(dq\) pushes a positive test charge at \(P\) directly along the axis, so we add scalar contributions. Let \(x\) be the distance from \(P\) to an element \(dq=\lambda\,dx\); it runs from \(a\) (near end) to \(a+L\) (far end):

\[ E=\int_a^{a+L}\frac{k\,dq}{x^2}=k\lambda\int_a^{a+L}\frac{dx}{x^2}=k\lambda\left[-\frac1x\right]_a^{a+L} \]
\[ E=k\lambda\left(\frac1a-\frac{1}{a+L}\right)=k\lambda\,\frac{L}{a(a+L)} \]

Since \(\lambda L=Q\), this becomes the clean result:

\[ \boxed{\,E=\frac{kQ}{a(a+L)}\,}\qquad\text{directed along the axis, away from the rod (for }+Q). \]
Sanity check — the far fieldIf \(a\gg L\), then \(a+L\approx a\) and \(E\approx \dfrac{kQ}{a^2}\): far away, the rod looks like a point charge. 🙂
Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. For the field \(E=\dfrac{kQ}{a(a+L)}\) of a finite rod, the limit \(a\gg L\) gives:
  • A\(E\to \dfrac{kQ}{a^2}\) — like a point charge
  • B\(E\to \dfrac{kQ}{aL}\)
  • C\(E\to 0\) faster than \(1/a^2\)
  • D\(E\to \dfrac{kQ}{L^2}\)
Answer A

If \(a\gg L\), then \(a+L\approx a\), so \(E\approx \dfrac{kQ}{a\cdot a}=\dfrac{kQ}{a^2}\) — the point-charge result.

MCQ
Q2. A rod of length \(L=0.20\ \text{m}\) has uniform charge \(Q=+4.0\ \text{nC}\). The field at a point \(a=0.10\ \text{m}\) beyond the near end (on the axis) is closest to:
  • A\(120\ \text{N/C}\)
  • B\(360\ \text{N/C}\)
  • C\(1.2\times10^{3}\ \text{N/C}\)
  • D\(3.6\times10^{3}\ \text{N/C}\)
Answer C
\[ E=\frac{kQ}{a(a+L)}=\frac{(8.99\times10^9)(4.0\times10^{-9})}{(0.10)(0.30)}=\frac{35.96}{0.030}\approx 1.2\times10^{3}\ \text{N/C} \]
FRQ
Q3. A thin rod of length \(L\) carries uniform total charge \(+Q\). Starting from \(dq=\lambda\,dx\), derive an expression for the electric field magnitude at a point \(P\) located a distance \(a\) from the near end, on the line of the rod. Show all steps.
Solution \(E=\dfrac{kQ}{a(a+L)}\)

Each element \(dq=\lambda\,dx\) at distance \(x\) from \(P\) contributes \(dE=\dfrac{k\,dq}{x^2}\) along the axis. With \(\lambda=Q/L\) constant and \(x\) running from \(a\) to \(a+L\):

\[ E=k\lambda\int_a^{a+L}\frac{dx}{x^2}=k\lambda\left[-\frac1x\right]_a^{a+L}=k\lambda\left(\frac1a-\frac1{a+L}\right)=\frac{k\lambda L}{a(a+L)}=\frac{kQ}{a(a+L)} \]

directed along the axis, away from the rod for positive \(Q\).

10

Thin Ring of Charge

Lecture 0468-3

A uniformly charged ring produces a beautiful axial field — zero at the center, peaking partway out, then fading like a point charge. It even drives simple harmonic motion.

The Problem & Setup

Find the field at point \(P\) on the axis of a uniformly charged ring (charge \(+Q\), radius \(a\)), a distance \(x\) from the center.

By symmetry, the radial components \(dE_y\) cancel in pairs (the \(dq\) on the opposite side cancels them). Only the axial component survives, with \(\cos\theta=\dfrac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+a^2}}\).

Result
\[ E=\int dE\cos\theta=\frac{kx}{(x^2+a^2)^{3/2}}\int dq=\boxed{\,\frac{kQx}{(x^2+a^2)^{3/2}}\,} \]
Three things to know about this result
  • Center: at \(x=0\), \(E=0\) (all contributions cancel).
  • Far field: for \(x\gg a\), \(E\approx \dfrac{kQ}{x^2}\) — a point charge again.
  • Maximum: \(E\) peaks at \(x=\dfrac{a}{\sqrt2}\approx 0.707\,a\).
Why a negative charge oscillates (SHM)For small \(x\), \((x^2+a^2)^{3/2}\approx a^3\), so \(E\approx \dfrac{kQ}{a^3}x\) — linear in \(x\). A negative charge then feels a restoring force \(F=-\dfrac{kQq}{a^3}x\) toward the center: a linear restoring force means simple harmonic motion about the center of the ring.
Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. The electric field at the exact center of a uniformly charged ring is:
  • Azero
  • Bmaximum
  • C\(kQ/a^2\)
  • Dinfinite
Answer A

Set \(x=0\) in \(E=\dfrac{kQx}{(x^2+a^2)^{3/2}}\): the numerator is zero. Physically, every \(dq\) is balanced by the \(dq\) directly across the ring, so the field cancels.

MCQ
Q2. Along the axis of a ring of radius \(a\), the field reaches its maximum at approximately:
  • A\(x=0\)
  • B\(x=a\)
  • C\(x=a/\sqrt2\approx 0.71a\)
  • D\(x=2a\)
Answer C

Setting \(dE/dx=0\) gives \(a^2-2x^2=0\Rightarrow x=a/\sqrt2\). This matches the graph's peak near \(x\approx 0.7\ \text{m}\) for \(a=1\ \text{m}\).

MCQ
Q3. On the graph above, the behavior of the curve at large \(x\) reflects which dependence?
  • A\(E\propto x\)
  • B\(E\propto 1/x^2\)
  • C\(E\propto 1/x\)
  • D\(E\) is constant
Answer B

Far away (\(x\gg a\)) the ring looks like a point charge, so the tail follows the inverse-square law \(E\approx kQ/x^2\). Near the origin the curve is instead linear in \(x\).

FRQ
Q4. A ring of radius \(a=0.10\ \text{m}\) carries uniform charge \(Q=+8.0\ \text{nC}\). (a) Find the axial field at \(x=0.10\ \text{m}\) from the center. (b) Explain why a small negative charge placed near the center and released will undergo simple harmonic motion.
Solution \(E\approx 2.5\times10^{3}\ \text{N/C}\)

(a) With \(x=a=0.10\): \(x^2+a^2=0.020\), and \((0.020)^{3/2}=2.83\times10^{-3}\).

\[ E=\frac{kQx}{(x^2+a^2)^{3/2}}=\frac{(8.99\times10^9)(8.0\times10^{-9})(0.10)}{2.83\times10^{-3}}\approx 2.5\times10^{3}\ \text{N/C} \]

(b) For small displacements \(x\ll a\), \((x^2+a^2)^{3/2}\approx a^3\), so \(E\approx \dfrac{kQ}{a^3}\,x\). The force on a charge \(-q\) is then \(F=-\dfrac{kQq}{a^3}\,x\): a restoring force proportional to displacement (\(F=-kx\) form), which is exactly the condition for simple harmonic motion about the center.

11

Electric Flux

Lecture 0469-1

Flux measures how much electric field "passes through" a surface — the essential bookkeeping we need before Gauss's Law.

Core Concepts

Electric flux is the amount of electric field passing through a defined area. For a uniform field through a flat area:

Electric flux (uniform field)
\[ \Phi_E=EA\cos\theta \]
  • \(\Phi_E\) = electric flux (scalar), units \(\text{N}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{C}\)
  • \(E\) = magnitude of the uniform field; \(A\) = area magnitude
  • \(\theta\) = angle between \(\vec E\) and the area's normal (the vector \(\vec A\))
Same form as workJust like \(W=Fd\cos\theta\), flux uses magnitudes and a \(\cos\theta\) that sets the sign. For a closed surface we sum over the whole surface: \( \Phi_E=\oint \vec E\cdot d\vec A.\) Field out of a closed surface is positive flux; field into it is negative flux.
Worked Example — net flux through a closed box
Example Uniform horizontal field through a triangular box

With \(\vec E\) pointing right, evaluate \(\Phi=EA\cos\theta\) on each face:

  • Back (face 1): normal points left, \(\theta=180^\circ\Rightarrow \Phi_1=-EA_1\) (field enters).
  • Bottom & the two triangular sides: normals are \(\perp\) to \(\vec E\), \(\theta=90^\circ\Rightarrow \Phi=0\).
  • Slanted top (face 5): its horizontal projection equals \(A_1\), so \(\Phi_5=+EA_1\) (field exits).
\[ \Phi_{\text{net}}=-EA_1+0+0+0+EA_1=0 \]
The big takeawayThe net flux through the closed box is zero because no charge is enclosed — every field line that enters also leaves. This is exactly the result Gauss's Law will guarantee.
Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. A flat area \(A\) is oriented so its normal is parallel to a uniform field \(E\). The flux through it is:
  • A\(EA\)
  • B\(0\)
  • C\(EA\sin\theta\)
  • D\(2EA\)
Answer A

Normal parallel to \(\vec E\) means \(\theta=0\), so \(\Phi=EA\cos0=EA\) — maximum flux.

MCQ
Q2. If that same area is rotated so its plane is parallel to the field (normal perpendicular to \(\vec E\)), the flux is:
  • A\(EA\)
  • B\(0\)
  • C\(EA\cos45^\circ\)
  • Dmaximum
Answer B

Now \(\theta=90^\circ\), so \(\Phi=EA\cos90^\circ=0\). No field lines pass through a surface lying edge-on to the field.

MCQ
Q3. A uniform field \(E=200\ \text{N/C}\) passes through a flat area \(A=0.50\ \text{m}^2\) with a \(60^\circ\) angle between \(\vec E\) and the area's normal. The flux is:
  • A\(100\ \text{N}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{C}\)
  • B\(87\ \text{N}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{C}\)
  • C\(50\ \text{N}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{C}\)
  • D\(0\)
Answer C

\(\Phi=EA\cos\theta=(200)(0.50)\cos60^\circ=(100)(0.5)=50\ \text{N}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{C}.\)

FRQ
Q4. A uniform field \(E=400\ \text{N/C}\) points in the \(+x\) direction. A cube of side \(0.20\ \text{m}\) is placed with its faces perpendicular to the axes. Find the flux through (a) the \(+x\) face, (b) the \(-x\) face, and (c) the net flux through the cube. (d) What does the net flux tell you about charge inside?
Solution

Each face area \(A=(0.20)^2=0.040\ \text{m}^2\).

(a) \(+x\) face: outward normal is \(+x\), \(\theta=0\): \(\Phi=EA=(400)(0.040)=+16\ \text{N}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{C}.\)

(b) \(-x\) face: outward normal is \(-x\), \(\theta=180^\circ\): \(\Phi=-16\ \text{N}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{C}.\)

(c) The other four faces have \(\theta=90^\circ\) (zero flux), so \(\Phi_{\text{net}}=+16-16=0.\)

(d) By Gauss's Law, zero net flux means zero net charge enclosed.

12

Gauss's Law — Point Charge

Lecture 0469-2

Gauss's Law links the flux through any closed surface to the charge it encloses — the first of Maxwell's equations.

Statement of Gauss's Law
Gauss's Law
\[ \Phi_E=\oint \vec E\cdot d\vec A=\frac{q_{\text{enc}}}{\varepsilon_0} \]
  • \(q_{\text{enc}}\) = net charge enclosed by the (closed) Gaussian surface
  • \(\varepsilon_0=8.85\times10^{-12}\ \dfrac{\text{C}^2}{\text{N}\cdot\text{m}^2}\) = permittivity of free space, with \(k=\dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\)
  • A Gaussian surface is an imaginary (or real) closed 3-D surface.
  • Choose its shape so \(\vec E\) is either \(\perp\) or \(\parallel\) to each face — then \(\cos\theta\) is just \(-1,0,\) or \(1\) and the integral is easy.
  • The net flux depends only on \(q_{\text{enc}}\), not on the surface's size or shape. If \(q_{\text{enc}}=0\), the net flux is zero.
Worked Derivation — recovering \(E=kq/r^2\)
Derivation Concentric sphere around a point charge

By symmetry \(E\) is constant over the sphere and parallel to \(d\vec A\), so \(\oint \vec E\cdot d\vec A=E(4\pi r^2)\). Setting this equal to \(q/\varepsilon_0\):

\[ E(4\pi r^2)=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}\ \Rightarrow\ E=\frac{q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2}=\frac{kq}{r^2} \]

Gauss's Law reproduces Coulomb's point-charge field — a powerful consistency check.

Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. The net electric flux through a closed surface depends on:
  • Athe size of the surface
  • Bthe shape of the surface
  • Conly the net charge enclosed
  • Dnearby charges outside the surface
Answer C

\(\Phi=q_{\text{enc}}/\varepsilon_0\). Only the enclosed charge matters. External charges and the surface's size/shape do not change the net flux.

MCQ
Q2. A Gaussian surface encloses no net charge, yet there is a strong field on the surface from a nearby external charge. The net flux is:
  • Azero
  • Blarge and positive
  • Clarge and negative
  • Dimpossible to determine
Answer A

\(q_{\text{enc}}=0\Rightarrow \Phi_{\text{net}}=0\). Field lines from an external charge that enter the surface also leave it; the local field can be large, but the net flux cancels.

MCQ
Q3. A point charge \(+5.0\ \mu\text{C}\) is enclosed by a Gaussian surface. The net flux through the surface is closest to:
  • A\(5.0\times10^{-6}\ \text{N}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{C}\)
  • B\(4.4\times10^{4}\ \text{N}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{C}\)
  • C\(5.6\times10^{5}\ \text{N}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{C}\)
  • DIt depends on the radius of the surface.
Answer C
\[ \Phi=\frac{q_{\text{enc}}}{\varepsilon_0}=\frac{5.0\times10^{-6}}{8.85\times10^{-12}}\approx 5.6\times10^{5}\ \text{N}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{C} \]

Independent of the surface's radius.

FRQ
Q4. Use Gauss's Law to derive the electric field a distance \(r\) from an isolated point charge \(q\). State the symmetry argument that lets you pull \(E\) out of the integral.
Solution \(E=\dfrac{kq}{r^2}\)

Choose a sphere of radius \(r\) centered on \(q\). By spherical symmetry, \(E\) has the same magnitude everywhere on the sphere and points radially — parallel to \(d\vec A\) — so \(\cos\theta=1\) and \(E\) is constant over the surface:

\[ \oint \vec E\cdot d\vec A=E\oint dA=E(4\pi r^2)=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0} \]
\[ \Rightarrow\ E=\frac{q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2}=\frac{kq}{r^2}. \]
13

Gauss's Law — Charged Plane

Lecture 0469-3

An infinite sheet of charge makes a strikingly simple field: uniform, and independent of distance. This is the foundation of the parallel-plate capacitor.

Single Infinite Plane

For an infinite plane with uniform surface charge density \(\sigma\), symmetry forces \(\vec E\) to point straight out, perpendicular to the plane (parallel components cancel). Use a cylindrical "pillbox" Gaussian surface piercing the plane, with two end-caps of area \(A\) equidistant from the plane.

Derivation Field of an infinite sheet

Flux exits both end-caps (\(2EA\)); the curved side contributes nothing (\(\vec E\parallel\) side). Enclosed charge is \(q_{\text{enc}}=\sigma A\):

\[ 2EA=\frac{\sigma A}{\varepsilon_0}\ \Rightarrow\ \boxed{\,E=\frac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0}\,} \]

Notice \(A\) cancels and \(r\) never appears: the field is uniform and independent of distance from the plane.

Two Parallel Planes (toward the capacitor)

Place a \(+\sigma\) plane and a \(-\sigma\) plane parallel to each other. Outside, the two fields cancel (\(E=0\)); between them, they add:

\[ E_{\text{between}}=\frac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0}+\frac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0} \]
Projectile analogyA charged particle in this constant field feels a constant force \(F=qE\) — so its motion mimics a mass in the uniform gravitational field near Earth's surface (projectile motion).
Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. How does the field of an infinite charged plane depend on the distance \(r\) from the plane?
  • A\(\propto 1/r^2\)
  • B\(\propto 1/r\)
  • C\(\propto r\)
  • DIt is independent of \(r\) (uniform).
Answer D

\(E=\sigma/2\varepsilon_0\) contains no \(r\). The field is uniform — the same magnitude at any distance from an infinite plane.

MCQ
Q2. Two infinite parallel planes carry \(+\sigma\) and \(-\sigma\). The field magnitude between the planes is:
  • A\(\dfrac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0}\)
  • B\(\dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}\)
  • C\(0\)
  • D\(\dfrac{2\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}\)
Answer B

Between the planes the two fields point the same way and add: \(\dfrac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0}+\dfrac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}\). Outside, they cancel to zero.

MCQ
Q3. An infinite plane has \(\sigma=2.0\times10^{-9}\ \text{C/m}^2\). The field magnitude just off the plane is closest to:
  • A\(18\ \text{N/C}\)
  • B\(110\ \text{N/C}\)
  • C\(230\ \text{N/C}\)
  • D\(1.1\times10^{4}\ \text{N/C}\)
Answer B
\[ E=\frac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0}=\frac{2.0\times10^{-9}}{2(8.85\times10^{-12})}\approx 113\ \text{N/C} \]
FRQ
Q4. Derive the field of an infinite plane of charge (surface density \(\sigma\)) using Gauss's Law. Describe your Gaussian surface and explain why the curved side contributes no flux.
Solution \(E=\dfrac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0}\)

Use a cylindrical pillbox piercing the plane, with end-caps of area \(A\) parallel to the plane. By symmetry \(\vec E\) is perpendicular to the plane, so it is parallel to the side wall — the side's \(\vec E\cdot d\vec A=0\) (field grazes it, \(\theta=90^\circ\)). Flux exits both caps: \(\Phi=2EA\). The enclosed charge is \(q_{\text{enc}}=\sigma A\). Gauss's Law:

\[ 2EA=\frac{\sigma A}{\varepsilon_0}\ \Rightarrow\ E=\frac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0}. \]
14

Gauss's Law — Charged Sphere

Lecture 0469-4

Outside any spherically symmetric charge, the field is identical to a point charge at the center. Inside, the answer depends on whether it's a conductor or insulator.

Outside the Sphere (\(r>a\))

For a sphere of radius \(a\) and total charge \(Q\), take a concentric Gaussian sphere of radius \(r>a\). It encloses all of \(Q\):

\[ E(4\pi r^2)=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0}\ \Rightarrow\ \boxed{\,E=\frac{kQ}{r^2}\,}\quad(r>a) \]
Inside the Sphere (\(r
TypeField inside (\(rWhy
Conductor\(E=0\)Charge resides on the surface; no charge is enclosed by an interior Gaussian sphere.
Insulator (uniform \(\rho\))\(E=\dfrac{kQr}{a^3}\)Only the charge within radius \(r\) is enclosed: \(q_{\text{enc}}=Q\dfrac{r^3}{a^3}\).
Insulator interior — quick derivation\(q_{\text{enc}}=Q\dfrac{r^3}{a^3}\), so \(E(4\pi r^2)=\dfrac{q_{\text{enc}}}{\varepsilon_0}\Rightarrow E=\dfrac{kQr}{a^3}\): the interior field grows linearly with \(r\), then matches \(kQ/r^2\) at the surface and falls off outside.
Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. Outside a uniformly charged sphere of total charge \(Q\), the field is the same as that of:
  • Aan infinite plane of charge
  • Ba point charge \(Q\) at the center
  • Czero everywhere
  • Da charged ring
Answer B

A concentric Gaussian sphere encloses all of \(Q\), giving \(E=kQ/r^2\) — identical to a point charge \(Q\) at the center.

MCQ
Q2. The electric field inside a charged solid conductor in electrostatic equilibrium is:
  • Azero
  • B\(kQ/r^2\)
  • C\(kQr/a^3\)
  • D\(\sigma/\varepsilon_0\)
Answer A

In a conductor at equilibrium, excess charge sits on the surface. An interior Gaussian sphere encloses no charge, so \(E=0\) inside.

MCQ
Q3. Inside a uniformly charged insulating sphere (\(r
  • A\(\propto r\) (linear, increasing)
  • B\(\propto 1/r^2\)
  • Cconstant
  • Dzero
Answer A

\(E=\dfrac{kQr}{a^3}\) inside the insulator — proportional to \(r\), rising from 0 at the center to \(kQ/a^2\) at the surface.

FRQ
Q4. A solid insulating sphere of radius \(a=0.10\ \text{m}\) carries total charge \(Q=+6.0\ \mu\text{C}\) spread uniformly. Find the field magnitude at (a) \(r=0.20\ \text{m}\) (outside) and (b) \(r=0.050\ \text{m}\) (inside).
Solution (a) \(\approx 1.3\times10^{6}\ \text{N/C}\); (b) \(\approx 2.7\times10^{6}\ \text{N/C}\)

(a) Outside, \(E=\dfrac{kQ}{r^2}=\dfrac{(8.99\times10^9)(6.0\times10^{-6})}{(0.20)^2}\approx 1.3\times10^{6}\ \text{N/C}.\)

(b) Inside the insulator, \(E=\dfrac{kQr}{a^3}=\dfrac{(8.99\times10^9)(6.0\times10^{-6})(0.050)}{(0.10)^3}\approx 2.7\times10^{6}\ \text{N/C}.\)

(Note the field peaks at the surface, \(r=a\), where both formulas give \(\approx 5.4\times10^{6}\ \text{N/C}\).)

15

Non-ideal Parallel Plates

Lecture 0670-2

Real capacitor plates aren't infinite. Understanding where the idealized picture breaks down sharpens your intuition for fields near conductors.

Ideal vs. Real

In the ideal model, two thin parallel plates of opposite, uniform charge are treated as infinitely large, giving a perfectly uniform field directed from the positive to the negative plate. In reality the plates are thin conducting sheets, very close together, usually with a thin insulating sheet between them.

Fringing & Two Common Errors

When plates are finite, two things change near the edges:

  1. Field direction at the plate: the field near a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium must be perpendicular to the conductor's surface — a naive drawing often violates this.
  2. Charge distribution: the charge is not perfectly uniform — it bunches up closer together near the edges and is more spread out in the middle.
Exam-Style Practice
MCQ
Q1. In the ideal parallel-plate model, the plates are treated as:
  • Apoint charges
  • Bspheres
  • Cinfinitely large sheets
  • Dinsulators
Answer C

Treating the plates as infinite makes the field uniform everywhere between them — the idealization that gives \(E=\sigma/\varepsilon_0\).

MCQ
Q2. The electric field immediately at the surface of a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium is always:
  • Aparallel to the surface
  • Bperpendicular to the surface
  • Czero just outside
  • Dat \(45^\circ\) to the surface
Answer B

Any parallel (tangential) component would push the free charges along the surface, contradicting equilibrium. So the field must be perpendicular — a key fact the naive non-ideal picture gets wrong.

MCQ
Q3. For finite (non-ideal) parallel plates, the charge on each plate is:
  • Aperfectly uniform
  • Bconcentrated only in the exact center
  • Cdenser near the edges than in the middle
  • Dzero everywhere
Answer C

On finite plates the charges crowd toward the edges and spread out in the middle, which is also where the fringing field bulges outward.

FRQ
Q4. Identify the two main errors in the commonly drawn "non-ideal" parallel-plate field, and describe what a better approximation looks like.
Solution

Error 1 — field direction: the naive drawing shows field lines that are not perpendicular to the conducting plates. But near a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium the field must be perpendicular to the surface.

Error 2 — charge distribution: it implicitly assumes uniform charge. On finite plates the charge is actually denser near the edges and more spread out in the middle.

Better approximation: field lines leave each plate perpendicularly, stay roughly uniform in the central region, and fringe (curve outward) near the edges, connecting from the positive plate to the negative plate.

Σ

Formula Reference Sheet

Unit 1

Every key equation and constant from Unit 1 in one place.

Constants
Coulomb constant
\(k=8.99\times10^{9}\ \dfrac{\text{N}\cdot\text{m}^2}{\text{C}^2}\)
Permittivity of free space
\(\varepsilon_0=8.85\times10^{-12}\ \dfrac{\text{C}^2}{\text{N}\cdot\text{m}^2}\)
Relation
\(k=\dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\)
Elementary charge
\(e=1.60\times10^{-19}\ \text{C}\)
Electron mass
\(m_e=9.11\times10^{-31}\ \text{kg}\)
Proton mass
\(m_p=1.67\times10^{-27}\ \text{kg}\)
Charge & Force
QuantityEquationNotes
Quantization of charge\(q=ne\)\(n\) must be an integer
Coulomb's Law\(F_e=\dfrac{kq_1q_2}{r^2}\)signs in: \(-\)=attract, \(+\)=repel
Conservation of charge\(\sum q_i=\sum q_f\)identical conductors share equally
Electric Field
SituationFieldNotes
Definition\(\vec E=\vec F_e/q\)units N/C
Point charge\(E=\dfrac{kq}{r^2}\)radial
Finite rod (on axis, from end)\(E=\dfrac{kQ}{a(a+L)}\)\(\to kQ/a^2\) when \(a\gg L\)
Ring (on axis)\(E=\dfrac{kQx}{(x^2+a^2)^{3/2}}\)max at \(x=a/\sqrt2\); \(0\) at center
Infinite plane\(E=\dfrac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0}\)uniform, independent of \(r\)
Between opposite planes\(E=\dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}\)\(0\) outside
Sphere, outside (\(r>a\))\(E=\dfrac{kQ}{r^2}\)like a point charge
Insulating sphere, inside (\(r\(E=\dfrac{kQr}{a^3}\)conductor: \(E=0\) inside
Charge Densities & Continuous Distributions
DensityDefinitionCharge element
Linear \(\lambda\)\(\lambda=Q/L\)\(dq=\lambda\,dx\)
Surface \(\sigma\)\(\sigma=Q/A\)\(dq=\sigma\,dA\)
Volume \(\rho\)\(\rho=Q/V\)\(dq=\rho\,dV\)
Field by integration\(\displaystyle \vec E=\int \frac{k\,dq}{r^2}\,\hat r\)
Flux & Gauss's Law
QuantityEquationNotes
Electric flux (uniform)\(\Phi_E=EA\cos\theta\)scalar; units \(\text{N}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{C}\)
Gauss's Law\(\displaystyle \oint \vec E\cdot d\vec A=\dfrac{q_{\text{enc}}}{\varepsilon_0}\)depends only on enclosed charge

Built from your Unit 1 lecture notes (Flipping Physics: Electric Charge, Coulomb's Law, Polarization, Conservation of Charge, Bohr Model, Balloon Charges, Electric Fields, Continuous Charge Distributions, Charged Rod, Thin Ring, Electric Flux, and Gauss's Law for point charges, planes & spheres).
